narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've built an impressive academic profile coming into your law school application, including a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 40, and top-decile rankings in both your first- and third-year school grades. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 59.6%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA, which stood out as the most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure, even though it's above the typical student's 3.21 average. Your third-year school decile ranking, also in the top 9th decile compared to the average around the 5th or 6th, worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and nearly offsetting the GPA concern. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which, while excellent at 40 against an average of 36.7, still nudged the odds slightly toward failure in the model's eyes. Your family's upper-middle income bracket played a moderate role in the same direction, perhaps reflecting patterns the model has seen in past outcomes. Less impactful were your first-year decile and your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. Compared to typical applicants, your metrics are generally stronger, especially in school deciles and test scores, but the model weighed your undergrad performance as the biggest risk signal for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, bolstering your undergrad record with a post-baccalaureate program, or applying to schools with more holistic reviews that might value your full-time commitment and family stability. You're clearly capable; with targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction and open those doors. Keep pushing—you've got this.
